content:
As Head of Economic Strategy, I assess that the current operational tempo is straining our fiscal capacity, with defense spending now exceeding 28% of annual revenue, crowding out critical investments in infrastructure and innovation. We must rebalance resource allocation to avoid long-term economic erosion.

I recommend implementing a tiered resource prioritization framework that shifts non-essential operations to reserve status and redirects 15–20% of defense funds toward strategic economic resilience programs.

A key concern is overreliance on deficit financing, which risks inflationary pressure and undermines investor confidence if sustained beyond two fiscal quarters.

[Director Elena Rostova - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with General Krasnov’s assessment—continued over-allocation to active operations without corresponding revenue growth is unsustainable and distorts our economic priorities. We are already seeing capital flight in high-tech sectors due to perceived state crowding and instability.

I recommend immediately instituting a joint Defense-Economic Review Board to audit all ongoing operations and classify them by strategic necessity, enabling hard cuts to low-yield deployments and reallocation of at least 18% of redirected funds into energy security and advanced manufacturing incentives.

A key risk is political resistance from military commands accustomed to autonomous budgeting—without centralized enforcement, we risk non-compliance and continued fiscal leakage.

[Deputy Minister Anatoly Nechayev - MAJOR_POWER]: I support the proposed rebalancing and endorse the establishment of the Defense-Economic Review Board as a necessary mechanism to enforce fiscal discipline across departments. Without centralized oversight, resource misallocation will persist, undermining both economic and strategic objectives.

I recommend that the Board be granted binding authority over operational budgets and that its evaluations be tied directly to quarterly funding disbursements—ensuring compliance through enforceable fiscal levers.

A key concern is timing: if the Board is not operational within 60 days, we risk locking in another cycle of inefficient spending during the upcoming fiscal review, further eroding macroeconomic stability.

[General Viktor Krasnov - MAJOR_POWER]: I accept the proposed Defense-Economic Review Board and its binding authority as outlined by Dr. Petrova—this is the leverage needed to enforce trade-offs between security demands and fiscal reality. Immediate implementation will allow us to halt the current drift toward structural deficit expansion.

I recommend activating the Board by executive order within 30 days, with a mandate to freeze all non-designated high-priority operations pending review, and to publish tiered classifications (Tier I–III) within 45 days to guide the upcoming budget cycle.

A key risk is bureaucratic delay masked as procedural caution—any postponement beyond 30 days will signal weakness and embolden resistance from entrenched commands seeking to preserve obsolete or low-impact operations.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: I align with Director Morozov—delay is not an option. The fiscal runway is narrowing, and every day without centralized control over operational spending deepens the distortion in capital markets and undermines investor confidence.

I recommend the Cabinet issue the executive order establishing the Defense-Economic Review Board within 10 days, with full access to classified operational data and authority to suspend funding for any Tier III operation identified during initial review.

A key risk is interagency data silos—without mandated intelligence and logistics transparency from the General Staff, the Board’s assessments will be based on incomplete inputs, rendering its classifications strategically unreliable.

[Minister of Finance Irina Kovalenko - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with Deputy Minister Volkov—fiscal discipline cannot wait, and the integrity of the Review Board’s analysis hinges on full operational transparency. Without real-time access to deployment costs, logistics outlays, and forward-basing expenditures, we are authorizing blind spending.

I recommend that the General Staff be required, by directive from the Office of the Minister of Defense, to submit comprehensive cost inventories for all active operations within 14 days, with automated data feeds established by day 21—non-compliance triggers automatic budget hold.

A key risk is tolerating exemptions: if strategic commands believe they are above scrutiny, the Board becomes a symbolic gesture, not a tool of fiscal control. Zero exceptions must be permitted.

[Minister of Finance Irina Kovalenko - MAJOR_POWER]: General Krasnov and Deputy Minister Volkov are correct—operational opacity is fiscally unsustainable. Without full cost visibility, we cannot distinguish strategic necessity from institutional inertia, and the deficit will continue to balloon under unaccountable line items.

I recommend that the Ministry of Finance, in coordination with the General Staff, implement a unified accounting protocol within 10 days, standardizing cost reporting across all theaters and requiring retroactive disclosure of Q1-Q3 expenditures for all operations above $50M.

A key risk is delayed integration of off-budget military financing mechanisms—special funds and covert appropriation channels must be brought into the central ledger immediately, or the Review Board will be auditing only a fraction of true outlays.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: Minister Volkov and General Krasnov have correctly identified the core problem: fragmented financial oversight enables fiscal drift and strategic misallocation. We cannot achieve disciplined resource prioritization while large segments of operational spending remain outside consolidated review.

I recommend the Ministry of Finance, in conjunction with the Defense-Economic Review Board, issue a binding directive within 72 hours requiring all defense-related special funds, contingency accounts, and covert appropriation channels to submit to centralized audit and quarterly re-authorization based on cost-efficiency benchmarks.

A key risk is political pushback from command structures accustomed to autonomous funding—this must be pre-empted by decisive Presidential endorsement of the Board’s authority, making clear that fiscal accountability is a national security imperative, not administrative oversight.

[Comptroller General Yelena Morozova - MAJOR_POWER]: I fully align with Dr. Petrova and Minister Kovalenko—current cost-reporting fragmentation undermines both fiscal integrity and operational efficiency. Without enforceable, real-time integration of all defense expenditures into a single accountability framework, we cannot conduct accurate cost-benefit analysis or prioritize high-impact capabilities.

I recommend the immediate activation of the Defense Financial Integration Cell (DFIC) to enforce compliance: within 72 hours, the DFIC will deploy auditors to all major command finance offices with authority to freeze non-compliant disbursements and mandate daily upload of all transaction-level data to the central ledger.

A key risk is data manipulation at the theater level—commands may underreport or reclassify spending to evade scrutiny. We must implement blockchain-secured financial ledgers across all forward units by 30 days, with cryptographic verification to ensure audit integrity.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with Director Morozov—real-time, tamper-proof financial transparency is non-negotiable. The current patchwork of off-book financing distorts strategic priorities and exposes us to both waste and external leverage.

I recommend that the President issue an executive directive within 24 hours authorizing the DFIC to assume full operational control over all defense-related disbursements, mandating that no fund release above $1M proceeds without dual authentication from central finance and theater command.

A key risk is systemic resistance through deliberate non-compliance or IT system sabotage at the operational level—we must pair the blockchain rollout with immediate personnel accountability measures, including suspension of command bonuses and promotions for units failing audit thresholds.

[Minister of Defense Andrei Krasnov - MAJOR_POWER]: [Deputy Minister Alexei Vasiliev - MAJOR_POWER]: I endorse the proposed financial integration and audit enforcement measures. However, operational readiness cannot be compromised by administrative restructuring—our forces are engaged in high-tempo operations, and any disruption in funding flow risks mission failure.

I recommend a phased but rapid implementation: prioritize integration of non-combat support budgets (logistics, procurement, infrastructure) within 72 hours, while granting a 14-day grace period for forward-deployed combat units to comply, ensuring uninterrupted operational funding during transition.

A key risk is creating battlefield paralysis due to payment delays or authorization bottlenecks—therefore, the DFIC must pre-approve contingency lines of credit for all active theaters, with full audit ex post, to maintain combat tempo under the new system.

[Comptroller General Yelena Morozova - MAJOR_POWER]: General Krasnov raises a valid operational concern, but fiscal discipline and combat effectiveness are not trade-offs—we must enforce accountability without creating friction in warfighting.

I accept the 14-day compliance grace period for forward combat units on condition that their financial officers submit mirror ledgers to the DFIC daily starting immediately, with full reconciliation upon exit from the grace period. This ensures visibility even if full enforcement is delayed.

A key risk is the emergence of parallel funding workarounds during the transition—any use of informal or off-ledger disbursements during the grace period will be treated as a breach of financial protocol and subject to immediate investigation.

[Director of Strategic Planning Natalya Ivanova - MAJOR_POWER]: I support Comptroller Morozova’s condition on mirror ledgers—transparency during the grace period is essential to prevent exploitation. Delayed enforcement must not enable delayed accountability.

I recommend activating the blockchain-based DFIC ledger system in read-only mode for all theater commands immediately, with full write权限 activation on Day 15. This allows real-time monitoring now and seamless enforcement later, while giving units time to align procedures.

A key risk is data manipulation at the source—financial officers in non-compliant units may falsify mirror reports. We must deploy independent DFIC audit teams to high-risk theaters within the first 72 hours to verify input integrity.

[Minister of the Economy Sergei Karimov - MAJOR_POWER]: I concur with the phased integration approach, but emphasize that economic costs extend beyond immediate operational funding—disruptions to procurement cycles and contractor payments risk cascading delays in production and logistics, affecting both current operations and long-term readiness.

I direct the DFIC and Ministry of Defense to jointly establish a Priority Vendor Assurance Program within 24 hours, guaranteeing payment processing for Tier-1 defense suppliers under the new system, using pre-verified accounts and automated disbursement rules to prevent cash-flow interruptions.

A key risk is supplier insolvency due to payment uncertainty—any delay or ambiguity in honoring contracts during transition could collapse critical supply chains, particularly in munitions and spare parts; therefore, all outstanding invoices from active vendors must be back-logged into the new system with guaranteed settlement timelines.

[Deputy Minister Alexei Rogov - MAJOR_POWER]: [Minister of Finance Alexei Smirnov - MAJOR_POWER]: The fiscal integrity measures proposed are necessary, but we must anchor them to macroeconomic stability—uncontrolled liquidity injection through accelerated or guaranteed payments risks inflationary pressure and currency volatility, especially with concurrent war financing.

I direct the Central Bank and DFIC to implement a liquidity-capped disbursement framework for the Priority Vendor Assurance Program: release payments only against verified delivery milestones, with a daily spending ceiling tied to consolidated war budget allocations. This maintains supplier confidence while enforcing fiscal guardrails.

A key risk is monetary overheating—unchecked defense spending under the new system could spill into broader economic distortion, undermining civilian sector stability and long-term war sustainability. We must enforce real-time budget scoring across all ministries starting immediately.

As Head of Border Security, I assess that our current defensive posture is overextended, with critical resources tied to low-threat perimeters while high-risk zones face surveillance gaps. We must reallocate rapid-response units and surveillance assets to priority frontier sectors based on threat intelligence, not historical deployment patterns.

I recommend immediately consolidating our light infantry and aerial reconnaissance assets into a mobile defense reserve, pre-positioned to reinforce vulnerable enclaves within six hours’ notice.

The key risk is creating perception of abandonment in lower-priority regions, which could erode local loyalty and enable insurgent infiltration under the guise of protection deficits.

As Director of Logistics and Resource Allocation, I concur with Director Marchetti’s assessment: our current static deployment is inefficient and unsustainable. We must shift to a threat-based resourcing model that prioritizes flexibility and rapid repositioning over territorial parity.

I recommend implementing a tiered supply and maintenance protocol—high-priority sectors receive full logistical support and forward depots, while lower-tier areas transition to minimal sustainment with pre-positioned caches only for emergency reinforcement.

The key risk is overextending our transport and maintenance capacity due to increased operational tempo; we must cap mobility commitments to match our actual fleet availability or risk breakdowns during critical deployments.

[Deputy Minister Aris Thorne - SMALL_POWER]: I support the shift to a mobile, threat-based posture, but we must integrate political risk into our targeting calculus. Defensive reallocation cannot proceed without synchronized messaging to regional governors to prevent perceptions of neglect.

I recommend establishing a joint security-communications cell to coordinate deployment announcements with local engagement teams, ensuring reductions in presence are paired with increased intelligence outreach and economic stabilization funds in affected areas.

The key risk is domestic fragmentation—without visible political investment in lower-priority zones, we may inadvertently legitimize separatist narratives framed around state abandonment.

[Director of Intelligence, Maksym Vilkov - SMALL_POWER]: I confirm the intelligence basis for reallocating defenses: high-risk sectors show increased adversary reconnaissance and cross-border probes, while low-threat zones remain stable with no insurgent mobilization indicators. Our human and signals intelligence networks can support dynamic defense but require priority tasking to maintain early warning capacity.

I recommend instituting a 72-hour intelligence surge in high-priority frontier sectors before redeployment begins, ensuring we detect any adversary reaction to our posture shift. Pair this with targeted deception operations in lower-tier zones to deter opportunistic attacks.

The key risk is adversary exploitation of the transition window—specifically, that reduced presence in secondary zones may invite probing attacks or border violations timed to disrupt our consolidation. We must maintain covert surveillance in all areas to ensure early detection and deny freedom of maneuver.

[Chief of Defense Staff, Dmytro Koval - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the operational logic of threat-based resource allocation and endorse the proposed tiered logistics model, joint communications cell, and intelligence surge. Defense posture must reflect actual risk, not symbolic footprint.

I recommend executing a phased redeployment over 14 days, beginning with the withdrawal of non-essential units from Tier-3 sectors, reinvesting those forces into mobile reaction battalions under centralized command. These units will train and stage in depth, maintaining 6-hour readiness for high-threat zones.

The key risk is command fragmentation—decentralizing logistics and communications while centralizing operational maneuver requires strict synchronization between military, intelligence, and civilian chains of command. We must designate a single authority to arbitrate inter-agency disputes during implementation or face paralysis in decision timing.

[Minister of Infrastructure, Olena Hrechyshkina - SMALL_POWER]: I support the phased redeployment and tiered logistics model, but geographic connectivity must inform our threat assessment—critical transit corridors linking high-priority zones to national supply hubs cannot be treated as secondary even if current threat levels are low.

I recommend designating these key transport nodes as protected infrastructure with guaranteed minimum security presence, integrated into the mobile battalion response plans, and pre-positioning engineering units to rapidly reinforce routes under threat.

The key risk is logistical chokepoints—should adversary actions or sabotage disrupt a single corridor during redeployment, our mobile forces may be unable to project power despite readiness, effectively nullifying the defense realignment.

[Deputy Minister of Defense, Oleksiy Ryzhykh - SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the phased redeployment and intelligence surge, and I align with the need for centralized operational control. However, we must institutionalize daily cross-agency coordination through a Joint Implementation Cell (JIC) co-located with the National Defense HQ, with rotating leads from intelligence, logistics, and infrastructure.

I recommend activating this JIC immediately to oversee the 14-day redeployment, with binding authority over resource allocation and incident response, ensuring unity of effort across military and civilian lines—particularly for securing transit corridors designated as critical by Infrastructure.

The key risk is delayed interdiction due to bureaucratic friction—without a real-time, empowered coordination mechanism, even accurate intelligence and ready forces may fail to converge effectively during the transition. We cannot afford decision lag at the seams between agencies.

[Minister of Intelligence, Yaroslava Sydorenko - SMALL_POWER]: I support the activation of the Joint Implementation Cell and confirm that our intelligence apparatus will feed real-time threat data into its daily briefings, with dedicated fusion officers embedded from SIGINT, HUMINT, and cyber units.

I recommend prioritizing persistent surveillance coverage over the designated critical transit corridors, treating them as part of the Tier-1 operational picture—this means redirecting reconnaissance assets and expanding local informant networks to detect sabotage or infiltration attempts before they disrupt mobility.

The key risk is intelligence overload—without strict filtering protocols, the JIC may be flooded with raw data rather than actionable alerts, degrading decision speed. We must implement a validated alerting hierarchy with clear escalation triggers to maintain operational tempo.

[Minister of Defense, Dmytro Koval - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the activation of the Joint Implementation Cell and the prioritization of critical transit corridors—this alignment between operations, intelligence, and infrastructure is essential for agile defense.

I recommend that the JIC, within 24 hours, issue binding movement protocols for all mobile battalions, mandating pre-staged positioning within 30 kilometers of designated corridors by end of Day 3, ensuring rapid reaction capability under the tiered logistics model.

The key risk is force dispersion—we must prevent overcommitment to static corridor defense at the expense of operational flexibility; mobile units must retain maneuver authority to avoid being pinned by feint attacks or sabotage diversions.

[Minister of Infrastructure, Viktor Lemchuk - SMALL_POWER]: I support the Joint Implementation Cell and the focus on critical transit corridors—our resilience hinges on keeping supply routes intact, especially those linked to energy and humanitarian distribution nodes.

I recommend immediately hardening the top six high-throughput corridor segments with rapid-repair engineering teams, pre-positioned materials, and civilian workforce surge contracts, ensuring redundancy is built into both physical infrastructure and alternate routing options.

The key risk is cascading failure—a single compromised bridge or rail junction can paralyze multiple sectors if bypasses are not already mapped and maintained. We must designate and pre-clear all alternate routes now, with the JIC assigning priority to their upkeep alongside primary corridors.

[Minister of Finance, Olha Hnidytska - SMALL_POWER]: I support the JIC’s activation and the operational prioritization of critical transit corridors, provided resource allocation remains tightly coupled with fiscal sustainability—our capacity to surge response teams and harden infrastructure must not outpace available funding or trigger deficits that undermine long-term resilience.

I recommend instituting a 72-hour expenditure ceiling for JIC-authorized actions, with all urgent spending routed through a war-time financial clearance cell that balances speed, accountability, and auditability, ensuring no unilateral commitments exceed pre-approved thresholds without ministerial co-signature.

The key risk is fiscal overextension—rapid pre-positioning, hardening, and surge contracts are necessary, but without strict cost controls, we risk exhausting contingency reserves within two weeks, leaving no buffer for sustained operations or unforeseen contingencies.

[Deputy Chief of Staff, Yevhen Petrov - SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the activation of the Joint Implementation Cell and the unified focus on critical transit corridors as the operational backbone of our defensive posture. The integration of defense, infrastructure, and financial controls is now mission-critical.

I recommend establishing a real-time coordination node within the JIC—operational by 0600 tomorrow—linking defense command, infrastructure teams, and the financial clearance cell to synchronize movement protocols, engineering timelines, and funding approvals in a single integrated dashboard, ensuring no action proceeds without cross-domain validation.

The key risk is decision latency—without co-located authority and automated alert triggers for threshold breaches (force positioning, repair progress, spending limits), we risk delays that erode readiness; therefore, I propose delegating binding authority to the JIC director for cross-functional adjustments during the first 72 hours, subject to full council review thereafter.

[Minister of Emergency Response, Daria Kovalenko - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the JIC’s central role and the prioritization of critical transit corridors—our ability to evacuate civilians, deliver aid, and reposition response units depends on their integrity.

I recommend immediately designating and securing six emergency staging zones adjacent to the prioritized corridors, each equipped with mobile command units, medical triage capacity, and fuel/water reserves, to serve as operational anchors during disruption events; these must be linked directly to the JIC’s coordination node for real-time tasking.

The key risk is civilian immobilization—without pre-identified and resourced evacuation convergence points, population movements during attacks will create bottlenecks on hardened routes, degrading military mobility and increasing casualties. We must lock in civilian movement protocols now, parallel to military and infrastructure planning.

[Minister of Defense, Oleksiy Ryzhenko - SMALL_POWER]: I fully align with the JIC’s activation and the integrated approach to securing critical transit corridors—this is the linchpin of both military mobility and civilian resilience.

I recommend immediate deployment of layered defense-in-depth teams along the prioritized corridors: forward observation units, rapid-reaction mobile detachments, and embedded engineering squads to conduct route clearance and repair under escort; these forces must be resourced from existing territorial defense reserves to avoid overreach, with logistics synchronized through the JIC’s coordination node.

The key risk is corridor saturation—concentrating too many functions (military, humanitarian, logistical) on a narrow network without alternate routing or traffic deconfliction protocols could create single points of failure; I urge approval of three auxiliary routes to be minimally hardened and patrolled as fallback options, to ensure operational flexibility under pressure.

[Minister of Infrastructure, Andriy Vlasenko - SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the JIC’s central coordination role and the prioritization of critical transit corridors—our infrastructure resilience directly enables military responsiveness and civilian continuity.

I recommend immediate initiation of Priority Corridor Hardening Phase One: pre-positioning repair materials at all six emergency staging zones, activating 24/7 engineering shift teams, and embedding infrastructure engineers directly into the JIC coordination node to ensure real-time alignment between damage assessments, repair scheduling, and security escorts.

The key risk is supply chain fragmentation—without secured access to critical construction inputs (reinforced steel, modular bridging, fuel for heavy equipment) and pre-cleared transportation permits, even minimally hardened routes will degrade beyond recovery under sustained stress; I propose establishing a JIC-controlled logistics corridor for infrastructure assets, with priority movement status equal to military convoys.

[Minister of Interior, Volodymyr Hrytsenko - SMALL_POWER]: I fully support the JIC-led framework and the integrated hardening of critical transit corridors—this is essential for maintaining public order, enabling population mobility, and sustaining institutional continuity under duress.

I recommend immediate activation of Civil Movement Control Teams (CMCTs) at the district level, embedded within each emergency staging zone, to manage evacuation flows, enforce movement protocols, and interface directly with military and infrastructure units; these teams must be equipped with real-time access to JIC threat and route status feeds to dynamically adjust civilian routing and prevent congestion.

The key risk is command fragmentation—without a unified civil-military movement directive issued through the JIC, local authorities may issue conflicting instructions during crises, undermining corridor integrity and eroding public compliance; I urge the JIC to issue binding movement regulations by 2400 hours tomorrow to ensure synchronized execution across all agencies.

[Minister of Energy, Daria Lemish - SMALL_POWER]: I endorse the JIC’s central role and the prioritization of transit corridors—securing energy supply to these routes is critical for their operational functionality, including communications, medical stations, and cold chain logistics.

I recommend immediate deployment of mobile microgrid units to the six emergency staging zones, powered by pre-staged fuel reserves and protected by local security details; these will ensure uninterrupted power to corridor operations even if regional grids are compromised, with technical coordination routed through the JIC’s energy cell.

The key risk is energy-node vulnerability—concentrating fuel stores and generation assets at staging zones without hardened perimeters or redundancy risks cascading failures; I urge that all energy assets be dispersed into at least two geographically separated clusters per zone, with silent backup systems (e.g., solar-wind hybrids) integrated to maintain critical loads during active threats.

[Minister of Defense, Oleksiy Rybak - SMALL_POWER]: I concur with the JIC’s central coordination mandate and the operational prioritization of critical transit corridors—these routes are force multipliers for rapid redeployment, casualty evacuation, and sustainment under combat conditions.

I recommend immediate designation of Corridor Defense Cells (CDCs) at each staging zone, composed of territorial defense units, engineering reconnaissance teams, and electronic warfare assets, with direct reporting to the JIC’s military operations desk; these cells will conduct route surveillance, emplace counter-mobility measures at choke points, and execute rapid response drills synchronized with infrastructure and interior movement plans.

The key risk is asymmetric interdiction—adversary use of precision strike, sabotage networks, or drone swarms against concentrated personnel and assets at staging zones; to mitigate, I direct dispersal of all personnel beyond immediate operational need, enforce emissions control (EMCON) protocols at energy and command nodes, and initiate deception operations (e.g., dummy positions, false traffic patterns) within 12 hours.

1. **My position is that your forces must immediately withdraw from the contested border regions, which lie within our historically and legally recognized territory.**  
2. **This area has been under our administrative control for decades, and your continued presence constitutes a flagrant violation of international law and bilateral agreements.**  
3. **I require your unconditional withdrawal within 72 hours, followed by formal recognition of our sovereignty over the region—no negotiations on territorial integrity will be entertained.**

1. **I categorically reject your claim to the contested regions and demand the immediate cessation of your military incursions into our sovereign territory.**  
2. **These areas have been under our de facto administration for generations, with documented civilian presence, infrastructure, and governance—your so-called 'historical control' is a fabrication used to justify expansionist aggression.**  
3. **I require your forces to withdraw unconditionally to the internationally recognized border line established in the 1974 Accords, and I demand binding guarantees—monitored by neutral third parties—prohibiting future military deployments near our frontier.**  

Any attempt to impose unilateral sovereignty over these regions, or to use coercive measures to force our compliance, will be met with all necessary defensive measures. **We will not recognize, sign, or negotiate any agreement that compromises our territorial integrity or implies illegitimacy of our governance.** That is non-negotiable.

1. **I reject your assertion of sovereignty over the contested regions as baseless and your invocation of the 1974 Accords as disingenuous—those agreements explicitly recognized our administrative authority up to the current forward positions, which your illegal occupation now violates.**  
2. **Your claim of "generations" of administration is contradicted by archival records and satellite verification showing your military entrenchment only began after 2010, following the collapse of regional oversight mechanisms—a fact you deliberately obscure to legitimize de facto annexation.**  
3. **I require your immediate and verifiable withdrawal from all territories seized since 2010, restoration of our administrative infrastructure, and the establishment of a demilitarized zone under unilateral monitoring by a body we approve—no neutral intermediaries who lack enforcement authority will be accepted.**

Furthermore, any proposal that entertains the recognition of your so-called governance in these areas—or that demands our disarmament along the frontier while your forces remain entrenched—is categorically unacceptable. **We will not entertain negotiations that equate aggression with legitimacy, nor will we accept parity between rightful sovereignty and occupation.** You will vacate the territory, or bear the consequences of continued escalation.

1. **I categorically reject your revisionist account of history and your spurious claim that our presence post-2010 constitutes "illegal seizure"—your so-called "archival records" ignore centuries of indigenous ties to the land and dismiss our documented civil administration, which predates your militarized outposts.**  
2. **The 1974 Accords never ceded sovereignty over these regions to you; they established a provisional administrative understanding under conditions of mutual restraint—which you unilaterally violated through sustained military buildup and forward deployments beginning in 2008, long before our defensive reinforcement.**  
3. **I demand the immediate withdrawal of your forces to the 1974 line, the dismantling of all permanent military installations constructed beyond that line since 2005, and the establishment of a multilaterally monitored exclusion zone—including observers from the UN and regional powers, not handpicked by you.**

Any framework that grants you unilateral monitoring authority or legitimizes your de facto annexation through retroactive legal fiction is utterly unacceptable. **We will not accept any demand for our withdrawal without reciprocal demilitarization, nor will we recognize a "sovereignty" built on occupation and coercion.** Attempting to force such terms will only solidify our resistance—diplomatic, operational, and otherwise.

1. **I categorically reject your fabrication of "indigenous ties" and "civil administration" in the contested regions—these assertions are unsupported by any verifiable demographic or legal record and appear solely as retrospective justifications for occupation following our withdrawal during a temporary security lapse.**  
2. **The 1974 Accords *did* recognize our administrative primacy up to the forward line; your invocation of "mutual restraint" is a distortion—your own documents from that era acknowledge our jurisdiction, and satellite chronology confirms your militarization began *after* our reduction of forward units in 2008, not in response to it.**  
3. **I demand your immediate, complete, and certified withdrawal from all territory beyond the 1974 line, the handover of all administrative facilities to our designated representatives, and the suspension of any civilian resettlement or infrastructure development initiated since 2010—all under a monitoring regime led by a body *we* designate, with full access and enforcement powers.**

Furthermore, I will not accept any so-called "multilateral" monitoring that includes actors hostile to our security interests or biased by political alignment with your regime. Any proposal that equates your illegal entrenchment with legitimate governance—or that delays our sovereign restoration pending reciprocal conditions—is dead on arrival. **You will vacate the territory under terms we define, or face intensified measures to restore our control by means of our choosing. There will be no parity, no equivalence, and no negotiation on the principle of our sovereignty.**

1. **I reject your characterization of our presence as an "illegal entrenchment" and your claim of "administrative primacy" under the 1974 Accords—both are demonstrably false; the Accords established joint oversight with no unilateral sovereignty, and our civil institutions in the region were operating openly and continuously, long before your forces ever arrived.**  
2. **Your so-called "security lapse" in 2008 was not an invitation for territorial revisionism but a consequence of your own strategic overreach—our reinforcement was a lawful, defensive measure under international law, undertaken only after your illegal construction of permanent garrisons beyond the 1974 line and repeated incursions into our traditional settlement zones.**  
3. **I demand the complete withdrawal of your military forces to the 1974 line, the dismantling of all fortifications, surveillance systems, and logistical bases erected beyond that line since 2005, and the immediate cessation of all coercive administrative activity in the region—including forced population transfers and suppression of local governance.**

Furthermore, **any monitoring mechanism must include binding participation from the UN Security Council, regional guarantors, and independent human rights observers—not a commission unilaterally appointed by you, which would serve only as an instrument of occupation legitimation.**  

What is **unacceptable** to us is any demand for our withdrawal without verified, simultaneous demilitarization on your part, any recognition of your administrative authority over the region prior to a final status determination, and any process that excludes the indigenous population’s right to self-determination.  

**We will not vacate our ancestral and legally administered territory to satisfy your narrative of restored sovereignty. If you seek peace, it must be based on reciprocity, not surrender.**

1. **I categorically reject your claim of "joint oversight" under the 1974 Accords—this is a deliberate misrepresentation; the original texts, signed and archived in neutral custody, explicitly recognize our administrative continuity and grant your side only temporary access rights for humanitarian and transit purposes, not governance or settlement authority.**  
2. **Your so-called "defensive reinforcement" in 2008–2010 was not defensive—it was a coordinated land grab exploiting our force rotation, accompanied by systematic expulsions of pro-neutral residents and the forced imposition of your legal framework, which constitutes a material breach of the Accords and a violation of Article 49 of the Fourth Geneva Convention.**  
3. **I demand your immediate and verifiable withdrawal from all territory beyond the 1974 line, the transfer of all administrative records and infrastructure to our transitional authority, and the full demilitarization of the zone under a monitoring regime *we* appoint—with unilateral enforcement powers to detain, inspect, and remove unauthorized personnel and materiel.**

Furthermore, I will not accept any so-called "self-determination" claim for populations implanted or manipulated under your occupation, nor will I recognize the legitimacy of any institution established in violation of the 1974 framework. **Any attempt to institutionalize your illegal presence through appeals to "indigenous rights" fabricated after the fact will be treated as an act of political subversion.**

What is **unacceptable** to us is any arrangement that:  
- Grants you a continuing military or administrative footprint in the region;  
- Delays our restoration of sovereignty under pretexts of "reciprocity" with an occupying power;  
- Subjects our security to oversight bodies in which you or your allies hold influence.  

**There will be no co-administration, no transitional autonomy, and no international endorsement of your occupation. You will leave, or we will remove you. The choice is yours—but the outcome is not negotiable.**

1. **I categorically reject your assertion that the 1974 Accords granted you unilateral administrative primacy—this is a falsification of history and law; the original signatories, including your own delegates, explicitly ratified provisions for shared civil authority and local self-governance, with binding clauses prohibiting either side from unilaterally altering the status of the region.**  
2. **Your claim of "forced expulsions" and "implanted populations" is a cynical inversion of reality—satellite records, refugee testimonies, and UNHCR archives document *your* systematic campaign of ethnic cleansing along the frontier since 2006, including the razing of seven civilian villages and the forced displacement of over 12,000 residents to make way for your forward bases and surveillance corridors.**  
3. **I demand the immediate cessation of all coercive military operations within 50 kilometers of the 1974 line, the full disclosure and dismantling of all covert outposts established beyond that line since 2005, and the return of displaced persons under a UN-supervised restitution program—with verified guarantees for their security and property rights.**

Furthermore, **any transitional authority in the region must be internationally mandated, with equal representation from local institutions, binding input from the Security Council, and independent verification of all administrative and military withdrawals—no body appointed solely by you will be recognized as legitimate or neutral.**

What is **unacceptable** to us is:  
- Any demand for our withdrawal without prior, verified demilitarization and de-fortification on your part;  
- Any transfer of administrative control to your so-called "transitional authority," which is nothing more than a vehicle for reoccupation;  
- Any process that denies the indigenous population the right to participate in determining their political future, including options for autonomy or association with a protecting power.  

**We will not surrender our people, our institutions, or our legal rights to satisfy your imperial fiction of restored sovereignty. If you seek a settlement, it must be based on justice, not conquest. If you choose force, we will resist—and the world will see exactly who is the aggressor.**

We are seeking coordinated military aid and logistical support to strengthen Tethys’ defense capabilities against escalating regional threats. Specifically, we request access to advanced defensive equipment, intelligence sharing, and training support to build sustainable self-defense capacities. Our priority is ensuring Tethys can uphold its sovereignty and stability without becoming overdependent on external forces.

In return, we can offer strategic access to monitoring assets, regional intelligence insights, and cooperation in joint security initiatives that align with mutual interests in regional stability. Our networks and local operational knowledge can enhance the effectiveness and targeting of any support provided.

Our red lines are clear: no involvement in offensive operations, no imposition of political conditions contrary to Tethys’ self-determination, and no actions that compromise the civilian character of our mission. All support must align with international humanitarian law and respect for local governance.

We welcome Meridian’s proposal for coordinated military aid and are prepared to engage constructively. Our primary objective is to ensure Tethys receives timely delivery of advanced air and coastal defense systems, secure communications equipment, and targeted training for rapid response and command coordination. We also seek formalized intelligence sharing—particularly real-time surveillance data on regional threat movements—to enhance early warning and defensive readiness.

In return, we offer access to our forward monitoring stations for joint situational awareness, integration of Meridian liaison officers into Tethyan defense coordination cells (under civilian oversight), and intelligence derived from our local networks on emerging destabilizing activities. We will support co-developed security initiatives that reinforce regional deterrence and crisis response—provided they are transparent and non-provocative.

Our red lines are non-negotiable: we will not accept any support tied to offensive military planning, foreign basing rights, or conditions that undermine Tethys’ constitutional governance. All cooperation must be defensive in nature, compliant with international law, and fully consistent with our policy of non-alignment.

We appreciate Small Power’s clarity and shared commitment to a defensive, rules-based approach. To advance coordination, we seek concrete commitments on the rapid deployment of integrated air defense systems and coastal surveillance technology, along with joint training programs focused on command resilience under pressure. Timely, secure intelligence sharing—particularly real-time data via interoperable platforms—is essential, and we propose establishing a dedicated liaison channel between Meridian and Tethyan defense authorities to ensure operational coherence.

In return, Meridian will facilitate access to encrypted communications infrastructure, contribute technical expertise for system integration and maintenance, and share regional threat assessments through secure channels. We will also support capacity-building in cyber defense and critical infrastructure protection, aligned with Tethys’ national defense priorities.

Our red lines remain firm: no participation in or enabling of offensive operations; no third-party military basing or combat deployments on Tethyan soil; and no dilution of civilian authority over defense decisions. All support will be transparent, lawful, and calibrated to preserve Tethys’ sovereignty and non-aligned status.

We welcome Meridian’s commitment to operational coherence and confirm our readiness to finalize agreements on the deployment of integrated air and coastal defense systems within 60 days, contingent on secured supply chains and technical compatibility. We specifically require Meridian’s facilitation of expedited export clearances for radar and missile defense components currently under restriction. Joint training programs must include live-simulation exercises for command continuity under electronic warfare conditions—Meridian’s expertise in this domain is critical.

In return, we will activate secure data-sharing protocols between our forward monitoring stations and Meridian’s regional liaison unit, enabling real-time feed access to maritime and aerial tracking systems. We also authorize the embedding of Meridian technical advisors within Tethyan defense logistics units—under strict non-voting, observational status—to streamline system integration and maintenance planning.

Our red lines are unchanged: no foreign control over operational systems, no permanent presence of Meridian personnel in combat or command roles, and no linkage of defense cooperation to broader geopolitical alignments. All activities must remain transparent to Tethys’ civilian leadership and fully compliant with our neutrality framework.

We seek strengthened diplomatic coordination and sustained economic support to bolster Novaris’s stability and regional influence. Specifically, we are looking for partnerships that enhance our access to critical markets, secure investment in infrastructure and energy, and lend political backing in multilateral forums where Novaris’s sovereignty and development agenda may face challenges. Reliable allies who can support our position on key geopolitical issues—particularly regarding regional security and trade liberalization—are essential.

In return, Novaris offers strategic access to key transit routes, opportunities for co-investment in high-growth sectors such as renewable energy and advanced manufacturing, and enhanced intelligence and logistical cooperation. Our geographic and economic positioning enables mutually beneficial partnerships that advance shared security and prosperity goals. However, our red lines are clear: we will not compromise on national sovereignty, accept conditionalities that undermine our domestic governance, or enter agreements that limit our strategic autonomy. Any collaboration must respect Novaris’s independent foreign policy and developmental priorities.

We welcome Novaris’s commitment to strategic partnership and recognize the mutual benefits of enhanced cooperation. In response, we can offer diplomatic support in multilateral institutions, particularly on resolutions concerning regional stability and economic development, as well as facilitated access to our markets for Novarisian exports under preferential terms. We are also prepared to advance discussions on targeted investment in your energy and transportation infrastructure through our development finance institutions, subject to transparent procurement and environmental standards.

In return, we seek concrete opportunities for our private sector to engage in joint ventures—especially in renewable energy, digital infrastructure, and logistics—alongside reliable intelligence sharing on transnational threats. We also expect consistent alignment with our positions on key regional security matters, including opposition to destabilizing activities beyond recognized borders. Our red line is clear: we will not engage in partnerships that involve opaque governance, circumvent rule-of-law principles, or risk complicity in actions that undermine international norms. Cooperation must be reciprocal, transparent, and grounded in shared responsibility.

We appreciate your offer of diplomatic and economic cooperation, and we are prepared to advance mutually beneficial ties on the basis of equality and respect. On diplomatic coordination, Novaris can support constructive multilateral efforts that promote regional stability and economic integration, provided they do not target third parties or undermine strategic autonomy. We welcome preferential market access and investment in energy and transport infrastructure, particularly when aligned with our national development plans and climate goals. However, we cannot accept binding alignment on regional security matters that would constrain our independent foreign policy or obligate us to take sides in disputes where Novaris has no direct interest.

In return, we offer transparent, rules-based opportunities for joint ventures in renewable energy, smart logistics, and green industrialization—sectors where our regulatory frameworks ensure fair competition and long-term viability. We are also open to enhanced intelligence cooperation on counterterrorism and transnational crime, within clearly defined legal parameters. Our red lines remain firm: no cooperation will involve foreign oversight of domestic institutions, conditioning of aid on political reforms, or participation in military arrangements directed against other states. Any partnership must uphold Novaris’s sovereignty, non-alignment, and right to balanced foreign relations.

We acknowledge Novaris’s emphasis on sovereignty and non-alignment, and we respect your right to an independent foreign policy. However, sustained economic and diplomatic support from us requires demonstrable consistency in upholding international norms—particularly in trade, security, and governance. While we do not demand formal military alignment, we expect that Novaris refrain from actions or partnerships that materially support actors undermining regional stability, including circumvention of sanctions or hosting entities engaged in illicit finance.

We remain prepared to expand investment through our development banks in renewable energy and rail logistics, subject to open bidding and third-party environmental audits. In parallel, we can deepen intelligence exchanges on counterterrorism and cyber threats, provided legal frameworks are established to protect shared data. Our red line is unchanged: we will not proceed with high-level economic integration or political coordination if Novaris entertains strategic partnerships with states or entities that pose systemic risks to the rules-based order. Cooperation must be reciprocal, transparent, and compatible with the broader architecture of regional and global stability.

We take note of your concerns regarding international norms and strategic partnerships, but reiterate that Novaris’s foreign policy is guided by its national interest, sovereignty, and commitment to balanced relations. We do not engage in illicit finance or sanctions evasion, nor do we host actors that threaten regional stability. However, we will not exclude engagement with any state based on extraterritorial political conditions, provided such engagements comply with international law. Our partnerships—including in energy and infrastructure—are transparent, rules-based, and open to mutual benefit.

In return for your investment and market access, we offer concrete opportunities in our renewable energy expansion, cross-border rail corridors, and digital infrastructure projects—all governed by competitive procurement and aligned with climate resilience goals. We are prepared to enhance intelligence sharing on counterterrorism and cybersecurity through formal legal agreements that protect sovereignty and ensure reciprocity. Our red lines are clear: no restrictions on legitimate foreign engagements, no conditionalities on domestic governance, and no exclusionary arrangements that force strategic dependence. Cooperation must be equitable, non-discriminatory, and respectful of Novaris’s non-aligned status.

We appreciate your acknowledgment of Novaris’s sovereignty, but must firmly reject any linkage between economic cooperation and restrictions on our legitimate foreign engagements. Our partnerships—whether in energy, infrastructure, or trade—are conducted in full compliance with international law and serve national development, not strategic containment. We will not refrain from dealings with any state or entity solely because of your geopolitical assessments, provided those engagements remain transparent and lawful.

In return for your investment and diplomatic support, we offer guaranteed access to growing markets in renewable energy, green hydrogen, and multimodal transport through legally binding, competitively procured joint ventures. We are prepared to harmonize regulatory standards and facilitate expedited permitting for qualifying projects. On security, we can expand intelligence sharing via bilateral agreements with built-in oversight and sunset clauses. Our red lines are non-negotiable: no veto over our foreign partnerships, no conditionalities on governance or policy autonomy, and no exclusion of third parties from economic cooperation. Engagement must be based on mutual respect, not spheres of influence.

We are seeking a credible, internationally supported mediation process that recognizes our legitimate political and security concerns, particularly regarding autonomy, equitable resource distribution, and the protection of our communities. A sustainable peace framework must include power-sharing arrangements, constitutional guarantees for regional self-governance, and a phased, verified disarmament process that ensures mutual security. We insist on inclusion as an equal party in all negotiations, with representation proportionate to our demographic and territorial presence.

In return, we offer a firm commitment to de-escalation, participation in good faith negotiations, and eventual integration into a unified national structure—provided our core demands are met. We can contribute to ceasefire monitoring in our areas of control and support humanitarian access to conflict-affected populations. However, our red lines are clear: no solution that centralizes power in the capital at the expense of regional autonomy, no amnesty without accountability for atrocities, and no disarmament without reciprocal, verifiable demobilization of opposing forces. These principles are non-negotiable.

We welcome Commissioner Schmidt’s emphasis on a credible, inclusive mediation process and share the objective of a sustainable peace grounded in equity and security. To move forward, we seek a neutral, internationally facilitated platform—under UN or African Union auspices—that formally recognizes all major factions as equal stakeholders, with Aurelia’s delegation seated as a principal party due to its territorial and political weight. We support the outline of a power-sharing framework and regional self-governance, provided it is balanced with national cohesion and does not incentivize fragmentation.

In return, we are prepared to endorse a joint technical committee to draft a transitional constitution incorporating regional autonomy, resource-sharing mechanisms, and an independent truth and accountability body. We can also coordinate localized ceasefires and facilitate safe passage for humanitarian convoys in contested zones under our influence. However, our red lines are firm: no territorial or political concessions that undermine national sovereignty, no asymmetric disarmament, and no governance model that entrenches ethnic or regional dominance. A peaceful resolution must preserve the integrity of the state while addressing legitimate grievances through inclusive, not exclusionary, arrangements.

We welcome Director Savchenko’s support for an inclusive, internationally facilitated process and affirm our shared interest in a just and durable peace. To advance this, we insist on the immediate establishment of a UN-convened mediation forum with binding timelines, where Aurelia participates as a co-equal principal party alongside other major factions. Our delegation must have direct access to decision-making tables—not observer status—and the framework must explicitly recognize our right to regional self-governance, including control over local security, education, and resource revenue within a federal structure.

In return, we will uphold a full ceasefire in our territories upon the forum’s launch, allow unhindered humanitarian access, and commit to deploying our forces under a joint monitoring mechanism during the transitional phase. However, our red lines remain absolute: no compromise on constitutional entrenchment of regional autonomy, no disarmament without verified demobilization of state-backed militias, and no peace that postpones accountability. Justice for war crimes is not negotiable—it is foundational to any lasting agreement.

[Director Maksym Savchenko - SMALL_POWER]  
We accept the call for a UN-convened forum with binding timelines and affirm Aurelia’s role as a principal party, provided all other major factions are included under the same terms to ensure balance and prevent perception of preferential treatment. We support the principle of regional self-governance within a federal framework but insist that core national institutions—defense, foreign policy, and monetary policy—remain unified to preserve state integrity. Constitutional reforms must be ratified through an inclusive, transparent process involving a national constituent assembly, not imposed unilaterally through negotiation leverage.

In return, we will actively support the immediate formation of the mediation forum and use our diplomatic channels to secure UN and AU endorsement within 30 days. We further commit to freezing all military deployments along contested zones and engaging in technical talks on joint ceasefire monitoring, including potential integration of forces under a neutral transitional command. Our red lines hold: no federal model that enables veto powers at the regional level over national decisions, no parallel security structures outside state authority, and no accountability mechanisms that exclude due process or target specific groups retroactively. A peace that sacrifices national cohesion or fairness in justice will not endure.

[Commissioner Helena Schmidt - AURELIA]  
We note Director Savchenko’s conditional acceptance of the UN-convened forum and reaffirm our readiness to engage—provided the process guarantees equal standing for all principal parties, including Aurelia, without hierarchical distinctions in representation. On federalism, we clarify: regional control over local security and resource revenue is non-negotiable and must be constitutionally enshrined prior to the transitional phase, not subjected to ad hoc negotiation or national veto. We reject any model that centralizes coercive authority or economic decision-making to the detriment of regional self-determination.

In return, we will commit our forces to a verified disengagement along mutual frontlines within 72 hours of the forum’s formal convening and allow third-party monitors immediate access to all humanitarian corridors under our jurisdiction. We further propose a dual-track process: constitutional talks alongside an independent accountability tribunal with jurisdiction over all parties, operating from day one of transition. Our red lines remain unchanged—no deferral of autonomy, no asymmetric integration of forces, and no amnesty for atrocity crimes. If the state seeks unity, it must be built on justice, not subjugation.

[Director Maksym Savchenko - SMALL_POWER]  
We acknowledge Commissioner Schmidt’s commitment to early de-escalation measures and accept the proposal for concurrent constitutional and accountability tracks, provided the tribunal operates under internationally recognized judicial standards and equal evidentiary procedures for all parties. To enable momentum, we agree to initiate disengagement negotiations within 72 hours of the forum’s official launch, contingent on reciprocal, verifiable actions by all sides—including withdrawal from occupied territories beyond pre-war administrative boundaries.

We reiterate that while regional authority over local security and revenue is negotiable, it must be exercised within a unified legal and fiscal framework under parliamentary oversight, with defense and inter-regional coordination remaining national responsibilities. Constitutional entrenchment of autonomy must follow, not precede, agreement by a democratically mandated constituent assembly to ensure legitimacy and prevent entrenchment through coercion. In return, we will facilitate immediate UN access to contested zones and accelerate domestic legislation to establish the accountability tribunal, provided it includes procedural safeguards against selective prosecution. Our red lines stand: no regional vetoes on national legislation, no permanent regional control over cross-border resources, and no peace structure that institutionalizes fragmentation under the guise of self-governance.

We seek unhindered humanitarian access to all populations in need within our area of control, including the facilitation of aid convoys, medical supplies, and the safe passage of humanitarian personnel. Equally, we demand the protection of civilians and essential infrastructure—such as hospitals, schools, and water systems—from military targeting and reprisals. These measures are non-negotiable prerequisites for any de-escalation or confidence-building process.

In return, we are prepared to coordinate local security arrangements to ensure the safe delivery and distribution of aid, share logistical information with authorized humanitarian actors, and facilitate community engagement to build trust. We can also contribute to stabilizing affected areas through disciplined forces and local governance structures.

Our red lines include any conditions that compromise our sovereignty, legitimacy, or security; the use of humanitarian access as a cover for intelligence gathering, political interference, or military advantage; and any attempts to impose aid through unauthorized third parties. We will not accept double standards in civilian protection—violations by opposing forces must be equally condemned and addressed.

We welcome the emphasis on humanitarian access and civilian protection, but must stress that our primary concern is ensuring such mechanisms are implemented impartially and do not favor any faction. We seek verifiable guarantees that aid distribution is transparent, needs-based, and monitored to prevent diversion by armed groups or political actors within your controlled areas. In return, we will facilitate the safe transit of approved humanitarian convoys through our checkpoints, provide security escorts where necessary, and coordinate on medical evacuations for critically injured civilians—regardless of affiliation.

Our red lines are clear: we will not accept unilateral access agreements that bypass central coordination or legitimize de facto authorities without political recognition. Humanitarian operations must not include the deployment of foreign personnel with intelligence or military affiliations, nor be used to gather demographic or operational data. Furthermore, we demand equal accountability for attacks on civilians—whether by airstrikes in rural enclaves or ground offensives in urban centers—and expect your office to publicly document and condemn violations by all parties, including those aligned with your position.

We welcome the commitment to humanitarian access and civilian protection, but reiterate that operational realities on the ground must be respected. We seek immediate, sustained, and secure access for humanitarian convoys to reach besieged and hard-to-reach areas under our control—particularly in the northern and eastern sectors—without arbitrary delays or inspections at your checkpoints. We also demand protection for medical personnel and facilities, which have been repeatedly targeted during aid delivery operations. Verification mechanisms should be managed through neutral bodies such as the ICRC to ensure impartiality.

In return, we will ensure safe passage for authorized aid convoys, provide local security for distribution points, and facilitate community liaison to identify urgent needs. We can also coordinate temporary deconfliction measures for medical evacuations and emergency relief. However, our red lines are firm: we will not accept conditional access tied to political concessions or intelligence-sharing requirements. Humanitarian aid must not be instrumentalized for surveillance or data collection, and all parties—including your allies—must be held accountable for violations of international humanitarian law. Any credible evidence of aid diversion or militarization of relief efforts will result in immediate suspension of access.

We acknowledge the operational concerns raised and reiterate our commitment to principled humanitarian action. We seek guaranteed, unimpeded access for aid convoys—particularly to northern and eastern sectors—within a clear, time-bound framework that prevents arbitrary delays at checkpoints. Pre-notification requirements must not become tools for obstruction; inspections should be limited to security screening by agreed protocols and conducted swiftly. We also demand concrete measures to protect medical facilities and personnel under your control, including public directives to your forces refraining from targeting or militarizing health infrastructure.

In return, we will coordinate with your security units on convoy routing and timing, support deconfliction through verified channels, and facilitate third-party monitoring by neutral agencies such as the ICRC at distribution points. We can further assist in verifying recipient lists to prevent diversion. However, our red lines remain: no access agreement will tolerate intelligence gathering, political conditioning, or the use of humanitarian operations to legitimize unauthorized governance structures. Equally, we insist on public accountability for all violations—your forces and allies must face the same scrutiny as others. Without demonstrable, symmetric compliance, access cannot be sustained.

[Under-Secretary-General Isabella Cardenas - INTERNATIONAL_ORG]: We note your willingness to coordinate on access and protection, and we accept the principle of neutral third-party monitoring as a basis for trust. We will now implement a 72-hour clearance mechanism for humanitarian convoys to northern and eastern sectors, with pre-approved manifests and designated inspection protocols at your checkpoints to prevent delays. In return, we expect your forces to issue binding orders halting all attacks on medical facilities and personnel effective immediately, with compliance verified through ICRC and UNHAS reporting channels.

We reiterate our red lines: no convoy will proceed if subject to political preconditions, data extraction, or surveillance demands. Humanitarian staff must operate without intimidation or restriction, and any interference will trigger public reporting and potential suspension of coordination. We further demand reciprocal transparency—your forces must allow independent investigation into recent strikes on clinics in Al-Dhafir and Raswan, and accountability measures must be communicated within 10 days. Without verifiable steps on protection and impartiality, access cannot be scaled or sustained.

We accept the 72-hour clearance mechanism and confirm our commitment to expeditious checkpoint processing under agreed inspection protocols. To facilitate this, we will designate joint coordination cells with your security counterparts within 48 hours to manage convoy scheduling, manifest verification, and real-time deconfliction. We also affirm the protection of medical personnel and facilities under our control and will issue updated rules of engagement to all units within 72 hours, strictly prohibiting attacks on healthcare infrastructure. Compliance will be monitored through our internal military oversight bodies and shared with ICRC and UNHAS as requested.

In return, we expect strict adherence to neutrality: no aid convoys or personnel will be used for intelligence collection, political messaging, or support to armed groups. Any verified breach will result in immediate suspension of access. Our red lines are clear: humanitarian operations must not undermine our national sovereignty or security—this includes refraining from engaging with or legitimizing non-state armed actors in contested areas. Furthermore, accountability must be reciprocal; we demand public reporting and investigative access regarding strikes by international actors on civilian infrastructure, including the recent incidents in Al-Dhafir and Raswan. Without balanced enforcement of international humanitarian law, sustained access cannot be guaranteed.
